document: Financial Advisory and Intermediary Services Act (FAIS) 37 of 2002

sections.heading: Definitions  and  application
sections.title: Exemptions for product suppliers in financial services
sections.summary: The registrar may exempt product suppliers authorized under other laws from submitting some information when applying for financial services authorization. Exemptions require the supplier to apply for exemption when submitting applications and comply with registrar's information requirements. Authorization remains separate from the supplier's authorization under other laws.
sections.dtia_summary: Exemptions allow certain entities to bypass data collection requirements, relevant for assessing data transfer obligations when suppliers are exempted from full registration.

sections.heading: CHAPTER VI
sections.title: Registrar's client education initiatives
sections.summary: The registrar may take steps to promote client education and awareness of the Ombud and other enforcement measures, including arrangements with financial services industry representatives, client and consumer bodies, and product suppliers to assist in public disclosure of information related to the Act.
sections.dtia_summary: This section allows the registrar to engage in client education and awareness activities, which could indirectly affect data transfer impact assessments by promoting transparency in financial data handling practices.

requirements.text: The registrar may take steps to promote client education and awareness of the Ombud and other enforcement measures.

sections.title: Amendment to require authorization for financial transactions
sections.summary: This amendment requires financial intermediaries to verify that a person they transact with has the required authorization or status as a financial services provider or representative under the Financial Advisory and Intermediary Services Act before engaging in transactions with them.
sections.dtia_summary: This section mandates verification of third-party authorization before transactions, which impacts data transfer by requiring validation of third-party data handlers.

sections.summary: This amendment modifies the requirements for financial exchanges to ensure they only engage with members or persons requiring approval under section 51 of the Financial Advisory and Intermediary Services Act, excluding those carrying on the business of a financial exchange.

sections.title: Advisory Committee on Financial Services Providers structure and functions
sections.summary: The Advisory Committee on Financial Services Providers consists of a chairperson and members including a medical schemes representative and representatives from product suppliers, financial services providers, and clients. The registrar is a non-voting member. Members serve for periods determined by the Minister and may receive remuneration. The Committee may investigate matters, call for assistance, and use the Commissions Act for summoning and examinations. The registrar handles administrative tasks and the Committee's expenses are paid from the Board's funds.
sections.dtia_summary: Committee provides oversight for financial services providers with specific composition and functions, including investigations and administrative support. Ensures regulatory compliance through structured governance mechanisms.

sections.summary: This amendment requires financial exchanges to immediately stop publishing or issuing advertisements, brochures, or similar documents related to financial instruments that are not correct statements of fact or are objectionable upon receiving a written request from the Registrar.

sections.title: Suspension of financial service licenses based on non-compliance
sections.summary: The registrar may suspend a financial service license if the licensee no longer meets section 8 requirements. Suspension must include informing the licensee of grounds, intended suspension period, and attached terms. The licensee receives a reasonable opportunity to respond before the registrar decides on suspension. Provisional suspension may occur under urgent circumstances to prevent substantial prejudice to clients or the public. During suspension, the licensee is deemed unauthorized.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires regulator to notify licensees before suspension, provide response opportunities, and maintain public transparency. Critical for assessing suspension risks in cross-border data transfers involving financial services providers.

sections.title: Withdrawal of financial service licenses for non-compliance
sections.summary: The registrar may withdraw a license if the licensee provided false information during application or has materially violated the Act. Withdrawn licenses are debarred from reapplying for a period specified by the registrar, which may be varied on good cause. The registrar must apply section 9 provisions to withdrawal decisions.

sections.title: Lapse of financial service licenses
sections.summary: A license lapses if the licensee becomes permanently incapable of business, is sequestrated, dies, is liquidated/dissolved, or voluntarily surrenders the license. The licensee must notify the registrar of lapsed license and reasons, and the registrar may publish lapsed licenses via Gazette or public media.

sections.title: Debarment of financial service representatives
sections.summary: Financial service providers must prohibit representatives who no longer meet competence requirements from rendering services, remove names from registers, and ensure client interests are protected. Debarred representatives must comply with registrar's requirements for reappointment after 30 days.

sections.title: Code of conduct for financial services providers
sections.summary: Authorised financial services providers must draft a code of conduct ensuring clients can make informed decisions, have their financial needs met, and receive appropriate services. The code must require providers to act honestly and fairly, maintain adequate resources and systems, seek client information, avoid conflicts of interest, and comply with all applicable laws. This applies to providers and their representatives.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires financial services providers to establish a code of conduct that ensures client protection and transparency, which impacts data transfer by mandating clear communication and safeguards for client information.

Providers must act with circumspection and treat clients fairly in situations of conflicting interests

Code must contain provisions on adequate and appropriate record-keeping

Code must contain provisions on suitable guarantees or professional indemnity or fidelity insurance cover

sections.title: Compliance officer appointment requirements
sections.summary: Authorised financial services providers with multiple key individuals or representatives must appoint compliance officers to monitor compliance with the Act, liaise with the registrar, and follow procedures for compliance. Compliance officers may be directors, members, auditors, or other qualified persons approved by the registrar. These officers must be approved by the registrar and providers must establish procedures for compliance reporting.

sections.title: Record retention for financial services providers
sections.summary: Authorised financial services providers must maintain records for a minimum of five years covering client transaction cancellations, complaints, compliance with section 8 requirements, non-compliance cases, and representative compliance. These records must be kept for regulatory oversight and dispute resolution purposes.

sections.title: Accounting and audit requirements for financial services
sections.summary: Authorised financial services providers must maintain full accounting records monthly and prepare audited financial statements annually. These statements must conform to generally accepted accounting practice, fairly represent financial position, and be submitted to the registrar within six months of the financial year end. Providers must also submit reports to the registrar confirming client funds are kept separate from business assets.

sections.title: Office of Ombud for Financial Services Providers
sections.summary: The Office of the Ombud for Financial Services Providers is established to handle complaints in a procedurally fair, informal, economical and expeditious manner. The Ombud must be impartial and consider complaints in light of contractual arrangements and the Act's provisions, ensuring equitable resolution.
sections.dtia_summary: Establishes a dedicated complaints mechanism for financial services providers, which impacts data transfer by providing a channel for client complaints regarding data handling practices.

sections.title: Ombud appointment and remuneration
sections.summary: The Board must appoint an Ombud qualified in law with adequate knowledge of financial services after consulting the Advisory Committee. The Ombud may appoint deputies, and their remuneration must be determined by the Board. The Ombud may resign with three months' notice, and the Board may remove them for misbehaviour, incapacity or incompetence after providing a hearing.

sections.heading: (1) The  funds of the Office consist of-
sections.title: Ombud's fund sources and financial management
sections.summary: The Ombud's funds originate from the Board's budget submissions approved by the Board and other sources. The Ombud must deposit all funds in a bank registered under the Banks Act, 1990. The Ombud may invest funds not immediately needed for expenses and must carry forward funds at the end of the financial year to the next year. The financial year ends on 31 March.

requirements.text: The Ombud must deposit all funds in a bank registered under the Banks Act, 1990
The Ombud may invest funds not required for immediate use

sections.source_quote: 22. (1) The funds of the Office consist of- (a) funds provided by the Board on the basis of a budget submitted by the Ombud and approved by the latter; and (b) funds accruing to the Office from any other source. (2) The Ombud must deposit all funds in an account opened with a bank registered under the Banks Act, 1990. (3) The Ombud must utilise such funds for the defrayal of expenses incurred in the performance of functions under this Act, and may invest funds which are not required for immediate use. (4) The financial year of the Ombud ends on 31 March in every year. (5) Funds standing to the credit of the Ombud in the account mentioned in subsection (2) at the end of the financial year, as well as funds invested under subsection (3), must be carried forward to the next financial year.

sections.heading: (1) The  Ombud is the  accounting officer in respect of all funds  received  and all
sections.title: Ombud's accounting responsibilities
sections.summary: The Ombud serves as the accounting officer for all funds received and payments made. The Ombud must maintain full and correct records of all funds, assets, liabilities, and financial transactions. The Ombud must prepare annual financial statements within three months after the financial year ends, and these statements must be audited by the Auditor-General.

sections.source_quote: 23. (1) The Ombud is the accounting officer in respect of all funds received and all payments made in respect of expenses incurred by the Office. (2) The Ombud as accounting officer must- (a) keep a full and correct record of all funds received and payments made and of all assets, liabilities and financial transactions of the Office; (b) as soon as is practicable, but not later than three months after the end of every financial year, prepare annual financial statements reflecting, with appropriate particulars, all funds received and payments made during, and all such assets, liabilities and transactions at the end of, the relevant financial year. (3) The records and financial statements mentioned in subsection (2) must be audited by the Auditor-General.

sections.title: Ombud's powers to manage office operations
sections.summary: The Ombud has broad powers to manage office operations, including hiring staff, acquiring property, entering agreements, insuring against losses, employing personnel, and obtaining professional advice. The Ombud may also delegate administrative functions to deputies and do anything necessary for the Ombud's objectives.

sections.source_quote: 24. The Ombud may for the performance of functions in the Office and as a charge against or for the benefit of the funds of the Office, as the case may be- (a) hire, purchase or otherwise acquire property, and let, sell or otherwise dispose of property so purchased or acquired; (b) enter into an agreement with any person for the performance of any specific act or function or the rendering of specific services; (c) insure the Office against any loss, damage, risk or liability; (d) employ persons to assist the Ombud, determine their terms of appointment and, subject to such conditions as may be determined by the Ombud, delegate or assign to any such employee, including a deputy ombud, any administrative function vesting in the Ombud in terms of this Part; (e) obtain such professional advice as may reasonably be required: and (f) in general, do anything which is necessary or expedient for the achievement of the objective of the Ombud.

sections.title: Board's rule-making authority
sections.summary: The Board may make rules after consulting the Advisory Committee, covering various aspects of complaints, including complaint categories, qualifying complainants, complaint types, complainant rights, provider responsibilities, complaint dismissal criteria, time limits, case fees, and administrative matters. sections.title: Ombud's complaint handling procedures
sections.summary: Upon receiving a complaint, the Ombud must determine if it meets requirements, otherwise officially receive it. Official receipt suspends prescription for three years from the date of receipt. The Ombud may decline investigations for complaints older than three years, where the complainant was unaware of the occurrence, or where court proceedings have been initiated. sections.source_quote: 27. (1) On submission of a complaint to the Office, the Ombud must- (a) determine whether the requirements of the rules contemplated in section 26(1)(a)(iv) in the case of any non-compliance, act in accordance with the rules made; (b) otherwise officially receive the complaint if it qualifies as a complaint. (2) Official receipt of a complaint by the Ombud suspends the running of prescription in terms of the Prescription Act, 1969, for the period after such receipt of the complaint until the complaint has either been withdrawn, or determined by the Ombud or the board of appeal, as the case may be. (3) The following jurisdictional provisions apply to the Ombud in respect of the investigation of complaints: (a) (i) The Ombud must decline to investigate any complaint which relates to an act or omission which occurred on or after the date of commencement of this Act but on a date more than three years before the date of receipt of such complaint by the Office. (ii) Where the complainant was unaware of the occurrence of the act or omission contemplated in subparagraph (i), the period of three years commences on the date on which the complainant became aware or ought reasonably to have become aware of such occurrence. whichever occurs first. (b) (i) The Ombud must decline to investigate any complaint if, before the date of official receipt of the complaint, proceedings have been instituted by the complainant in any Court in respect of a matter which would constitute the subject of the investigation. (ii) Where any proceedings contemplated in subparagraph (i) are instituted during any investigation by the Ombud, such investigation must not be proceeded with. (c) The Ombud may on reasonable grounds determine that it is more appropriate that the complaint be dealt with by a Court or through any other available dispute resolution process, and decline to entertain the complaint. (4) The Ombud must not proceed to investigate a complaint officially received, unless (a) has in writing informed every other interested party to the complaint of the receipt thereof; (b) is satisfied that all interested parties have been provided with such particulars as will enable the parties to respond thereto; (c) has provided all interested parties the opportunity to submit a response to the complaint. (5) The Ombud- (a) may, in investigating or determining an officially received complaint, follow and implement any procedure (including mediation) which the Ombud deems appropriate, and may allow any party the right of legal representation; (b) must, in the first instance, explore any reasonable prospect of resolving a complaint by a conciliated settlement acceptable to all parties; (c) may, in order to resolve a complaint speedily by conciliation, make a recommendation to the parties, requiring them to confirm whether or not they accept the recommendation and, where the recommendation is not accepted by a party, requiring that party to give reasons for not accepting it: Provided that where the parties accept the recommendation, such recommendation has the effect of a final determination by the Ombud, contemplated in section 28(1); (d) may, in a manner that the Ombud deems appropriate, delineate the functions of investigation and determination between various functionaries of the Office; (e) may, on terms specified by the Ombud, mandate any person or tribunal to perform any of the functions referred to in paragraph (d). (6) For the purposes of any investigation or determination by the Ombud, the provisions of the Commissions Act, 1947, regarding the summoning and examination of persons and the administering of oaths or affirmations to them, the calling for the production of books, documents and objects, and offences by witnesses, apply with the necessary changes.

sections.title: Ombud's final determination on unresolved complaints
sections.summary: The Ombud must make a final determination if a complaint remains unresolved or a recommendation from section 27(5)(c) is unaccepted. This determination may dismiss the complaint, uphold it wholly or partially with compensation for financial prejudice, issue directions for actions, or make any order a court could. Monetary awards may include interest rates and dates set by the Ombud, with maximum awards for specific complaints determined by the Board through rules. Costs may be awarded against complainants for improper conduct or unreasonable delays, and these awards bear interest. Interest rates for awards cannot exceed what a court would have awarded. The Ombud must write, sign, and send determinations to the registrar and parties, and if an appeal is lodged, send copies to the board of appeal. Final determinations are treated as civil judgments and must be noted by courts. Appeals are limited to the board of appeal based on complexity or likelihood of a different outcome. Writs of execution may be issued for monetary awards, and other determinations must be implemented like court orders after a two-week period.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes the Ombud's authority to resolve complaints with binding outcomes that function as court judgments, requiring detailed documentation and specific procedures for monetary awards and appeals. These outcomes directly impact cross-border data transfers if the Ombud's determinations involve financial data from international entities.

sections.title: Exclusions from Act application
sections.summary: This section specifies that the Act does not apply to certain entities and individuals, including authorized users, clearing houses, central securities depositories, exchange-licensed entities, collective investment scheme managers, liquidators, trustees of specific trusts, minors' guardians, and others, provided they comply with requirements under relevant laws.

requirements.text: The provisions of this Act do not apply to the rendering of financial services by any authorized user, clearing house, central securities depository or participant as defined in section 1 of the Securities Services Act, 2002, or exchange licensed under section 10 of that Act
The provisions of this Act do not apply to the rendering of financial services by a manager as defined in section 1 of the Collective Investment Schemes Control Act, 2002

The provisions of this Act do not apply to the rendering of financial services by a person performing functions referred to in section 13B of the Pension Funds Act, 1956, if such person complies with the requirements and conditions contemplated in that section

The provisions of this Act do not apply to the rendering of financial services by a person carrying on the business referred to in section 58 of the Medical Schemes Act, 1998, if such person complies with the requirements contemplated in that section

The provisions of this Act do not apply to the rendering of financial services by the executor, administrator or trustee of any deceased or insolvent estate, or a person acting on behalf of such executor, administrator or trustee
The provisions of this Act do not apply to the rendering of financial services by the curator of a person under curatorship, or a person acting on behalf of such curator

The provisions of this Act do not apply to the rendering of financial services by the liquidator or judicial manager of a company in liquidation or under judicial management, or a person acting on behalf of such liquidator or judicial manager

The provisions of this Act do not apply to the rendering of financial services by the trustee of an inter vivos trust as defined in section 1 of the Trust Property Control Act, 1988, not being a business trust created for the purpose of profit-making achieved through the combination of capital contributed by the beneficiaries of the trust and through the administration or management of the capital by trustees on behalf of and for the benefit of the beneficiaries

The provisions of this Act do not apply to the rendering of financial services by the parent, tutor or guardian of a minor, or a person acting on behalf of such parent, tutor or guardian, unless the financial services are rendered as a regular feature of any such person's business

sections.heading: A record  purporting to have
sections.title: Admissibility of financial records in criminal proceedings
sections.summary: A record made or kept in the ordinary course of a financial exchange, member, or recognized clearing house business is admissible as evidence in criminal proceedings if certified by the public prosecutor. This applies to the person carrying on the business or any other person involved in the proceedings. The record must be certified to be correct by the public prosecutor to be admissible.
sections.dtia_summary: This section ensures that certified financial records can be used as evidence in criminal cases, which may impact data admissibility in cross-border legal proceedings involving financial institutions.

requirements.text: A record made or kept in the ordinary course of the business of a financial exchange or member or recognized clearing house
The record must be certified to be correct by the public prosecutor
The certified record is admissible in criminal proceedings

sections.source_quote: A record purporting to have been made or kept in the ordinary course of the carrying on of the business of a financial exchange or the business of a member, or of a recognized clearing house [or the business of a person approved in terms of section 51] a copy of or an extract from such record certified to be correct by the public prosecutor shall on its mere production by the public prosecutor in any criminal proceedings under this Act or any other law or the common law against the person who carries on the business in question or any other person, be admissible in evidence and be prima facie proof of the facts contained in such record, copy or extract.
